attributes.stem:
A 54-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for post-operative review four weeks after undergoing a laparotomy for gastric carcinoma. She made a good recovery before discharge. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a painless swelling over the surgical site that gradually appeared about two weeks post-operatively (Image). She reports the swelling is enlarging but is not causing any discomfort. She denies fever or abdominal pain. The discoloured swelling is non-compressible, non-tender, and does not change with coughing or straining.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of a large haematoma expanding in the subcutaneous tissue. The yellowish discolouration gives an important clue to the diagnosis as it is highly suggestive of a subcutaneous haematoma. Haematomas are usually painless and non-tender.

A wound abscess commonly presents with localising pain associated with an erythematous, tender swelling and purulent exudate, which is not the case in this patient. Abscesses are also commonly associated with systemic symptoms such as fever and chills.

Metastatic recurrence is an unlikely diagnosis. It is highly improbable for metastatic gastric carcinoma to present as a rapidly growing, painless swelling within four weeks of surgery. Patients with metastatic recurrence are more likely to present with constitutional symptoms of fatigue, weight loss, and night sweats.

An incisional hernia is an important differential diagnosis to consider. However, incisional hernias typically present as swellings that are accentuated by coughing or straining. It is also unlikely for an incisional hernia to present with the skin discolouration seen in the image.

This could be a post-operative seroma, except that the yellowish discolouration points towards a bleed (haematoma) rather than a leakage of serum into the wound site.
learningPoints: Subcutaneous haematoma typically presents with a swelling associated with yellowish discolouration.
Wound haematomas are usually painless and non-tender.
references: Yao K, Bae L, Yew WP. Post-operative wound management. Australian Journal for General Practitioners. 2013;42:867-70. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/december/post-operative-wound-management

Sandy-Hodgetts K, Ousey K, Howse E. Top ten tips: management of surgical wound dehiscence. Wounds International. 2017;8(1):11-5. Available at: https://woundsinternational.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2023/02/wint_8-1_11-14.pdf
Incisional Hernia. The Johns Hopkins University [online]. 2022; cited 2022 Sep 1. Available at: https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/health/conditions-and-diseases/hernias/incisional-hernia
